scenario: You get a text message: "URGENT: Your Social Security Number has been suspended due to suspicious activity. Call 1-800-555-4823 immediately or face arrest."
explanation: Government agencies never suspend your Social Security number by text message, and they never threaten arrest. This is a classic government impersonation scam. Delete the message immediately.
redFlags: Threatening arrest
Urgent pressure to act now
Unknown phone number
Government agencies never text about this

scenario: Your bank sends you this email: "Dear customer, we noticed unusual activity. Please verify your account at www.mybank-secure-login.net within 24 hours to avoid suspension."
explanation: Real bank emails come from the bank's official domain (like @yourbank.com), not lookalike websites ending in .net with extra words. The fake web address is the biggest red flag here.
redFlags: Suspicious web address (not the real bank)
Urgency and time pressure
Threatening account suspension
Real banks never ask you to log in via email links

scenario: You receive a call: "Hello, I'm calling from Microsoft Support. We have detected a virus on your computer. Please allow me remote access to fix it for a $99 fee."
explanation: Microsoft never calls you out of the blue about viruses on your computer. This is the 'tech support scam' — one of the most common scams targeting seniors. Hang up immediately.
redFlags: Unsolicited call from 'Microsoft'
Asking for remote access to your computer
Asking for payment over the phone
Microsoft does not monitor individual computers

scenario: Your friend sends a WhatsApp message: "Hi! I'm locked out of my account and need you to forward me the 6-digit code you just received on your phone. It's urgent!"
explanation: This is account takeover fraud. Scammers hack your friend's account, then use it to trick you. The 6-digit code is YOUR login code for WhatsApp or another service. Never share it.
redFlags: Request to forward a verification code
Urgency
Even trusted contacts can be hacked
No legitimate service asks for codes this way

scenario: You get a text from your electricity provider saying: "Your bill payment was successful. Your next bill of Rs. 1,847 is due on March 15. View your account at powercompany.com."
explanation: This looks like a normal billing notification. It doesn't ask for immediate payment, doesn't create urgency, and links to a real company website. Always verify the sender's number matches your provider.

scenario: You receive a phone call: "Congratulations! You have been selected to receive a free medical alert device worth $400. We just need your Medicare number to ship it to you at no cost."
explanation: This is Medicare fraud. Scammers use your Medicare number to bill for fake services. There are no truly free medical devices given over the phone. Hang up and call Medicare directly if concerned.
redFlags: Asking for Medicare or insurance number
Something for free to create goodwill
Unsolicited call
Too good to be true offer

scenario: Your grandson calls you: "Grandma, I'm in trouble — I had an accident and I'm in jail. I need $2,000 in Amazon gift cards right now. Please don't tell mom and dad, I'm so embarrassed."
explanation: This is the 'grandparent scam'. Scammers impersonate grandchildren to create emotional panic. No real emergency ever requires payment in gift cards. Hang up and call your grandson directly on his known phone number.
redFlags: Asking for gift cards (never a real payment method for emergencies)
Secrecy — asking you not to tell family
Emotional manipulation and urgency
Police and jails never accept gift card payments

scenario: You get a text: "Amazon: Your package #TRK2847 could not be delivered. Update your delivery address: amzn-delivery-update.com"
explanation: Real Amazon delivery texts come from Amazon's official systems and link to amazon.com. The web address 'amzn-delivery-update.com' is a fake site designed to steal your password and credit card.
redFlags: Web address is not amazon.com
Creating urgency about a package
Amazon never asks you to re-enter delivery info via SMS link

scenario: Your doctor's office calls to confirm: "This is a reminder call from Dr. Sharma's clinic. Your appointment is tomorrow, Tuesday at 10:30 AM. Press 1 to confirm or 2 to cancel."
explanation: This is a standard appointment reminder call. It doesn't ask for personal information, payment, or account numbers. It only asks you to confirm or cancel an existing appointment you booked.

scenario: A WhatsApp message from an unknown number: "Hi aunty, this is my new number. I lost my old phone. Can you send Rs. 5000 via UPI? I will return it tomorrow. Very urgent."
explanation: This is UPI fraud using social engineering. Scammers claim to be a relative with a new number to create trust. Always verify by calling the person on their old number or through a family member. Never send money to unverified numbers.
redFlags: Unknown number claiming to be family
Urgency and time pressure
Immediate money request
Asks for UPI transfer — hard to reverse
